input_row.title: [LEGACY] Ensure That Microsoft Defender for DNS Is Set To 'On'
input_row.description: [ NOTE: As of August 1, 2023 customers with an existing subscription to Defender for DNS can continue to use the service, but new subscribers will receive alerts about suspicious DNS activity as part of Defender for Servers P2.] Microsoft Defender for DNS scans all network traffic exiting from within a subscription.
input_row.rationale: DNS lookups within a subscription are scanned and compared to a dynamic list of websites that might be potential security threats. These threats could be a result of a security breach within your services, thus scanning for them could prevent a potential security threat from being introduced. Impact: Enabling Microsoft Defender for DNS requires enabling Microsoft Defender for your subscription. Both will incur additional charges, with Defender for DNS being a small amount per million queries.
input_row.audit: Audit from Azure Portal 1. Go to Microsoft Defender for Cloud 2. Under Management, select Environment Settings 3. Click on the subscription name 4. Select the Defender plans blade 5. Ensure Status is set to On for DNS. Audit from Azure CLI Ensure the output of the below command is Standard az security pricing show -n 'DNS' --query 'PricingTier' Audit from PowerShell Get-AzSecurityPricing --Name 'DNS' | Select-Object Name,PricingTier Ensure output of PricingTier is Standard Audit from Azure Policy If referencing a digital copy of this Benchmark, clicking a Policy ID will open a link to the associated Policy definition in Azure. If referencing a printed copy, you can search Policy IDs from this URL: https://portal.azure.com/#view/Microsoft_Azure_Policy/PolicyMenuBlade/~/Definitions • Policy ID: bdc59948-5574-49b3-bb91-76b7c986428d - Name: 'Azure Defender for DNS should be enabled'
input_row.remediation: Remediate from Azure Portal 1. Go to Microsoft Defender for Cloud. 2. Under Management, select Environment Settings. 3. Click on the subscription name. 4. Select the Defender plans blade. 5. Select On under Status for DNS. 6. Select Save. Remediate from Azure CLI Enable Standard pricing tier for DNS: az security pricing create -n 'DNS' --tier 'Standard' Remediate from PowerShell Enable Standard pricing tier for DNS: Set-AzSecurityPricing -Name 'DNS' -PricingTier 'Standard' Default Value: By default, Microsoft Defender for DNS is not enabled. References: 1. https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/pricing/details/defender-for-cloud/ 2. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/security/benchmark/azure/baselines/dns- security-baseline 3. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/defender-for-cloud/defender-for-dns- alerts 4. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/defender-for-cloud/enable-enhanced- security 5. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/defender-for-cloud/alerts-overview 6. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/security/benchmark/azure/security-controls-v3- network-security#ns-10-ensure-domain-name-system-dns-security 7. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/security/benchmark/azure/mcsb-logging-threat- detection#lt-1-enable-threat-detection-capabilities Additional Information: [ NOTE: As of August 1, 2023 customers with an existing subscription to Defender for DNS can continue to use the service, but new subscribers will receive alerts about suspicious DNS activity as part of Defender for Servers P2.]
input_row.impact: Enabling Microsoft Defender for DNS requires enabling Microsoft Defender for your subscription. Both will incur additional charges, with Defender for DNS being a small amount per million queries.
input_row.reference: 1. https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/pricing/details/defender-for-cloud/ 2. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/security/benchmark/azure/baselines/dns- security-baseline 3. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/defender-for-cloud/defender-for-dns- alerts 4. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/defender-for-cloud/enable-enhanced- security 5. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/defender-for-cloud/alerts-overview 6. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/security/benchmark/azure/security-controls-v3- network-security#ns-10-ensure-domain-name-system-dns-security 7. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/security/benchmark/azure/mcsb-logging-threat- detection#lt-1-enable-threat-detection-capabilities Additional Information: [ NOTE: As of August 1, 2023 customers with an existing subscription to Defender for DNS can continue to use the service, but new subscribers will receive alerts about suspicious DNS activity as part of Defender for Servers P2.]

first_opinion.response:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
The audit can be fully verified via Azure Resource Manager APIs and CLI commands as specified in the audit section of the control. The presence of specific Azure CLI and PowerShell commands in the audit indicates the property can be programmatically queried to confirm compliance.

**PROGRAM NAME:** azure_defender_for_dns_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
The automated program would check the status of Microsoft Defender for DNS using the following methods:

1. **Azure CLI Command:**
   - Command: `az security pricing show -n 'DNS' --query 'PricingTier'`
   - This command retrieves the current pricing tier of Microsoft Defender for DNS.

2. **PowerShell Command:**
   - Command: `Get-AzSecurityPricing --Name 'DNS' | Select-Object Name, PricingTier`
   - This command similarly checks the Pricing Tier for DNS via PowerShell.

3. **Expected Outputs:**
   - Both commands should return `Standard`, indicating that Defender for DNS is enabled.

4. **Pass/Fail Criteria:**
   - If the output from either command indicates `Standard`, the check is considered a Pass (indicating compliance).
   - If it returns anything other than `Standard` (such as `Basic` or `Not Applicable`), it would be considered a Fail (indicating non-compliance).

The automation would involve iterating over all subscriptions to ensure that Defender for DNS is enabled across all relevant environments within the Azure account.

second_opinion.response:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. The compliance check for Microsoft Defender for DNS can indeed be verified using Azure Resource Manager APIs, Azure CLI, and PowerShell commands. These tools allow for programmatic querying of the relevant settings, making the process suitable for automation.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8

- The automation details are mostly accurate. The Azure CLI and PowerShell commands provided are correct for checking the pricing tier of Microsoft Defender for DNS. However, it is important to note that the command `az security pricing show -n 'DNS'` and `Get-AzSecurityPricing --Name 'DNS'` will only return the current pricing tier, which indirectly indicates whether Defender for DNS is enabled. The expected output of `Standard` is correct for compliance.
- The program name `azure_defender_for_dns_enabled` follows a logical naming convention, although it could be more descriptive by including the resource type, such as `azure_security_pricing_dns_enabled`.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Clarification on Output Interpretation:** It would be beneficial to clarify that the `Standard` pricing tier indicates that Microsoft Defender for DNS is enabled, while other tiers or the absence of a tier might indicate non-compliance.
- **Subscription Iteration:** Ensure that the automation script iterates over all relevant subscriptions within the Azure tenant, as this is crucial for comprehensive compliance checking.
- **Error Handling and Logging:** Incorporate error handling and logging mechanisms to capture any issues during the execution of the commands, which can aid in troubleshooting and audit trails.
- **Documentation Update:** Given the note about the service's transition to Defender for Servers P2, it would be prudent to update the documentation to reflect any changes in how DNS security is managed or reported in the future.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment is well-founded based on the current capabilities of Azure CLI and PowerShell for querying security pricing tiers. The approach aligns with Azure's best practices for automated compliance checks.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:** Modify

- While the overall approach is sound, I recommend enhancing the automation script to include comprehensive logging, error handling, and clear documentation on interpreting the command outputs. Additionally, consider updating the program name to `azure_security_pricing_dns_enabled` for better clarity and consistency with naming conventions.

final_decision.response:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:** Both AI opinions agree that the compliance check for Microsoft Defender for DNS can be automated using Azure CLI and PowerShell commands. The commands provided (`az security pricing show -n 'DNS' --query 'PricingTier'` and `Get-AzSecurityPricing --Name 'DNS'`) are capable of programmatically verifying the status of Defender for DNS by checking the pricing tier. This aligns with the audit steps outlined in the compliance control data, confirming that automation is feasible and accurate.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:** azure_defender_dns_status_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Azure Resource/Service: Microsoft Defender for DNS
- API/CLI Method: `az security pricing show -n 'DNS' --query 'PricingTier'`, `Get-AzSecurityPricing --Name 'DNS'`
- Properties to Check: PricingTier
- Pass Criteria: The command output should return `Standard`, indicating Defender for DNS is enabled.
- Fail Criteria: Any output other than `Standard` indicates non-compliance.

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:** There were no disagreements between the AI opinions; both concurred on the feasibility of automation.
